title: 25 Hidar — St. Tekle Haymanot & The Assembly
saints: St. Tekle Haymanot of Ethiopia (Monthly Commemoration)
St. Clement of Rome, Pope and Martyr
narrative:
The twenty-fifth day of Hidar carries the monthly Tekle Haymanot — the Light of Ethiopia, the great apostolic saint of the South — and places him beside the Departure of St. Mercurius (Abu Seifein) and the feast of Clement of Rome in an assembly that maps the apostolic commission across its Ethiopian, Egyptian, and Roman expressions. Tekle Haymanot in the month of the Apostolic Vesting is Tekle Haymanot as the great exemplar of what the vesting produces: a life whose interior formation was so thorough and so complete that the external apostolic activity — the missionary journeys, the community formations, the monastic foundations, the intercession for the departed — flowed from it without forcing. In Hidar, Tekle Haymanot is not primarily the ascetic of the one-legged vigil; he is the vested apostle of the Ethiopian highlands, the one in whom the interior Tiqimt and the exterior Hidar are seamlessly unified, who could not stop praying and could not stop going because for him the two activities had become indistinguishable.

The Departure of St. Mercurius — Abu Seifein, the Father of Two Swords, who appeared on the fourth day of this same month in his living capacity — is now commemorated in his dying, completing the arc that his month-opening vesting began. The soldier who was vested with the martyr's commission has now fulfilled it; the two swords — of military service and of spiritual warfare — have been laid down at the Throne that the Twenty-Four Elders surround, and the man who carried them is received by the Host whose Captain he served. In the month of the Apostolic Vesting, Mercurius's departure is the completion-feast that the twenty-first day's Michael feast pointed toward: the soldier vested by the Captain, carrying the commission faithfully to the end, and arriving at the destination that the commission always had in view.

St. Clement of Rome — the Apostolic Father whose First Letter to the Corinthians is the oldest surviving Christian document outside the New Testament — returns on the twenty-fifth day of Hidar in a different capacity from his previous appearances. Here he is the Roman voice of the apostolic vesting: the sub-apostolic bishop who received the commission from the eyewitnesses and passed it, in his letter and in his martyrdom, to the next generation. His letter's authority — which he exercised not in the name of a legal office but in the name of the apostolic tradition he had received — is the apostolic vesting's literary form: the written transmission of the commission from those who received it directly to those who will carry it forward. In Hidar's apostolic month, Clement is the testimony that the vesting can travel in a letter as surely as it travels in the laying on of hands.

Tekle Haymanot, Mercurius's departure, and Clement together on the twenty-fifth day present the apostolic vesting in its Ethiopian, martyric, and literary completions. The great highland monk completes his vesting in the unity of prayer and mission. The soldier-martyr completes his vesting in the sword's surrender at the Throne. The Roman bishop-martyr completes his vesting in the letter that carries the commission across two millennia. All three are forms of the same completion — the apostolic life fully lived, the commission fully honored, the Sender fully served — and the community that receives these three on the twenty-fifth day is being shown what its own completion might look like, in whatever form the particular vesting of the particular life requires.
